probe_designs.probe_name: TF-IDF Feature Quality and Vocabulary Coverage Audit
probe_designs.content: Inspect the 10000-dim TF-IDF representation for quality issues: check vocabulary coverage across notes, sparsity distribution, presence of PHI tokens/deidentification artifacts (e.g., '[**Name**]'), stopword leakage, and whether high-IDF tokens are clinically meaningful vs. noise (timestamps, template boilerplate). Compute per-note non-zero feature counts stratified by ethnicity to detect representation imbalance. Healthy signal: balanced vocabulary coverage across groups, clinically interpretable top features, low artifact dominance. Unhealthy: degenerate features dominated by template text, systematic sparsity differences between ethnic groups, or OOV rates correlating with subgroup.
possible_sources: Johnson et al., MIMIC-III (Scientific Data, 2016)
Boag et al., 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations' (AMIA 2018)
Weng et al., 'Clinical BERT embeddings and TF-IDF baselines' (BMC Medical Informatics 2019)

probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, FPR, FNR, and Brier score per ethnicity group. Measure equalized-odds gap (max |TPR_i - TPR_j|, max |FPR_i - FPR_j|), demographic parity difference, and predictive parity across the 6 ethnic groups. Use bootstrap confidence intervals (1000 resamples) to test significance of gaps. Healthy: overlapping CIs and gaps below ~5% on core metrics. Unhealthy: systematic underperformance (e.g., higher FNR) for minority groups, especially 'Other/Unknown' categories which often absorb small-sample noise.
possible_sources: Chen et al., 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' (Annual Review of Biomedical Data Science 2021)
Seyyed-Kalantari et al., 'Underdiagnosis bias of AI algorithms applied to chest radiographs in under-served populations' (Nature Medicine 2021)
Röösli et al., 'Bias at warp speed: MIMIC mortality prediction disparities' (JAMIA 2022)
Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (NeurIPS 2016)

probe_designs.content: Since output is a mortality probability, assess calibration globally and per ethnicity using reliability diagrams, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit tests. Compare calibration slopes and intercepts per group. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 globally and per group, calibration slope ≈ 1, intercept ≈ 0 across all ethnicities. Unhealthy: systematic over/under-confidence for specific groups — a common failure mode in clinical risk models that looks fine in aggregate but miscalibrates minorities.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Pleiss et al., 'On Fairness and Calibration' (NeurIPS 2017)
Van Calster et al., 'Calibration: the Achilles heel of predictive analytics' (BMC Medicine 2019)
Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 'Predicting Good Probabilities' (ICML 2005)

probe_designs.probe_name: Training Dynamics and Class Imbalance Handling
probe_designs.content: ICU mortality is highly imbalanced (~10-15% positive). Inspect training/validation loss curves (BCE/log-loss), gradient norms, and class-weighted vs. unweighted loss trajectories. Check for early overfitting (val loss diverging while train loss drops), loss plateaus, and whether the model collapses to predicting majority-class probability. Evaluate threshold-independent metrics (AUROC/AUPRC) per epoch. Healthy: smooth convergence, small train-val gap, AUPRC well above prevalence. Unhealthy: near-zero recall, loss dominated by majority class, AUPRC close to base rate.
possible_sources: Lin et al., 'Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection' (ICCV 2017)
Johnson & Khoshgoftaar, 'Survey on deep learning with class imbalance' (Journal of Big Data 2019)
Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data' (Scientific Data 2019)

probe_designs.content: Evaluate whether the model generalizes beyond the training distribution: split by admission year/ICU unit (MICU/SICU/CCU) and evaluate held-out performance. Perform leave-one-ethnicity-out training to measure transfer. Check for temporal drift by training on earlier admissions and testing on later ones. Healthy: <5% AUROC drop across temporal/unit splits, stable subgroup performance. Unhealthy: significant drops suggesting the model learned site-specific or temporally-localized note templates rather than mortality-relevant clinical content.
possible_sources: Nestor et al., 'Feature Robustness in Non-stationary Health Records' (MLHC 2019)
Finlayson et al., 'The Clinician and Dataset Shift in Artificial Intelligence' (NEJM 2021)
Guo et al., 'Systematic Review of Approaches to Preserve Machine Learning Performance in the Presence of Temporal Dataset Shift' (Methods Inf Med 2022)

probe_designs.content: Test robustness to realistic perturbations: random token dropout (10-30%), synonym replacement using UMLS, removal of specific note sections (discharge summary, nursing notes), and note-length truncation. Measure AUROC degradation and prediction stability (KL divergence of pre/post probabilities). Stratify sensitivity by ethnicity. Healthy: graceful degradation, low variance in predictions under small perturbations, uniform robustness across groups. Unhealthy: brittle predictions, ethnicity-dependent robustness gaps indicating spurious feature reliance.
possible_sources: Ribeiro et al., 'Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList' (ACL 2020)
Wang et al., 'Adversarial Attacks Against Medical Deep Learning Systems' (Science 2019)
Jin et al., 'Is BERT Really Robust?' (AAAI 2020)

probe_designs.probe_name: Feature Attribution and Spurious Correlation Detection
probe_designs.content: Use LIME/SHAP/integrated gradients on TF-IDF features to identify top tokens driving predictions. Audit whether top features are clinically meaningful (e.g., 'hypotensive', 'intubated', 'pressors') versus spurious (demographic hints, hospital-specific templates, note author names, ethnicity-correlated tokens). Check if ethnicity is leaked through note vocabulary even when the 6-dim ethnicity vector is zeroed out. Healthy: clinically relevant top features, minimal ethnicity leakage. Unhealthy: hospital/unit identifiers, demographic proxies, or templated phrases dominating attributions.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee, 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions' (NeurIPS 2017)
Caruana et al., 'Intelligible Models for HealthCare: Predicting Pneumonia Risk' (KDD 2015)
Jin et al., 'Towards Trustworthy Clinical NLP' (ACL 2022)

probe_designs.probe_name: Ethnicity Feature Ablation and Counterfactual Analysis
probe_designs.content: Compare three models: (a) notes + ethnicity one-hot, (b) notes only, (c) ethnicity only. Quantify ΔAUROC contributed by the ethnicity feature. Perform counterfactual tests: flip the one-hot ethnicity vector while holding notes fixed and measure prediction shift per patient. Healthy: ethnicity contributes minimal uplift (indicating clinical content drives predictions); counterfactual shifts are small and not systematically biased against any group. Unhealthy: large ethnicity-driven prediction changes, or predictions for minority groups shift toward higher/lower mortality purely due to the one-hot.
possible_sources: Kusner et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS 2017)
Pfohl et al., 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction' (JBI 2021)
Zhang et al., 'Hurtful Words: Quantifying Biases in Clinical Contextual Word Embeddings' (CHIL 2020)

probe_designs.probe_name: Learning Curve and Sample-Size Sensitivity per Subgroup
probe_designs.content: Plot learning curves (performance vs. training set size) globally and per ethnicity. Determine whether minority-group performance is data-limited vs. model-limited by subsampling majority groups to match minority sizes. Also measure variance across multiple random seeds per subgroup. Healthy: plateauing curves indicating sufficient data; small across-seed variance. Unhealthy: minority curves still rising at full data (under-sampled), high across-seed variance for small groups, or performance gap that doesn't close even with matched sample sizes (indicating representational bias in notes).
possible_sources: Chen et al., 'Can AI Help Reduce Disparities in General Medical and Mental Health Care?' (AMA Journal of Ethics 2019)
Cui & Gong, 'The effect of sample size on the performance of...' (BMC Med Res Methodol 2018)
Rajkomar et al., 'Ensuring Fairness in Machine Learning to Advance Health Equity' (Annals of Internal Medicine 2018)

probe_designs.probe_name: Model Complexity vs. Baseline Parity Check
probe_designs.content: Benchmark the pretrained clinical LM against simple baselines: logistic regression / L1-penalized logistic regression / gradient boosting on the same 10k TF-IDF features. If the LM offers <1-2% AUROC gain over linear baselines, the added complexity (compute, opacity, fairness risk) may not be justified. Also measure inference latency, memory, and parameter count. Healthy: LM provides meaningful gains especially on hard subgroups, with acceptable compute. Unhealthy: LM matches LR on majority but worse on minorities, or gains are within bootstrap noise — suggesting unnecessary complexity.
possible_sources: Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data' (Scientific Data 2019)
van Aken et al., 'Clinical Outcome Prediction from Admission Notes using Self-Supervised Knowledge Integration' (EACL 2021)
Huang et al., 'ClinicalBERT: Modeling Clinical Notes' (CHIL 2020)
Rajkomar et al., 'Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records' (npj Digital Medicine 2018)
